message.content: Adversaries may exploit the PATH environment variable to hijack execution flow by placing malicious programs in directories listed earlier in the PATH sequence. This technique leverages the sequential search behavior of Windows when executing binaries from scripts or the command line. By positioning a malicious executable with the same name as a legitimate system program (e.g., cmd, PowerShell, or Python) in a higher-priority directory, attackers ensure their payload is executed instead of the intended system binary. This can lead to unauthorized code execution and potential system compromise. Common tools like Empire and PowerSploit include modules to identify and exploit such path interception opportunities. Mitigation strategies include restricting write permissions to directories in the PATH variable, monitoring and validating changes to the PATH environment variable, and employing application whitelisting to ensure only trusted binaries are executed.
